title: A Game of Thrones The Board Game

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get A Game of Thrones set up. Unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The board shows the continent of Westeros divided into land areas with white borders and sea areas with red borders. You will also see several tracks along the edges: the Supply track, the Victory track, three Influence tracks (Iron Throne, Fiefdoms, and King's Court), and the Wildlings track. The Round track is also on the board, numbered 1 through 10.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Wildling Deck and Threat Token
walkthrough.text: Find the 9 Wildling cards. These are the large cards with a distinct back design. Shuffle them into a face-down deck and place it on the space provided at the top of the game board. Then find the Wildling Threat token and place it on the 2 position of the Wildlings track. The wildlings start at threat level 2, and this will rise during the game as Westeros cards with Wildling icons are drawn.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Westeros Decks
walkthrough.text: Find the 30 Westeros cards. Separate them into three decks by the Roman numeral printed on each card: Deck I, Deck II, and Deck III. Each deck has 10 cards. Shuffle each deck separately and place them face-down next to the game board. These decks drive the events at the start of each round, and they include critical events like Supply, Mustering, and Clash of Kings.

walkthrough.title: Place the Neutral Force Tokens
walkthrough.text: Collect the 14 Neutral Force tokens. These represent independent areas that resist player control. Each token is marked with a player range number. For a six-player game, place only the tokens marked 4-6. For five players, place tokens marked 4-6 and 4-5. For four players, place those plus the tokens marked 4. For three players, place the tokens marked 3 (these have a tilde symbol instead of a strength value, meaning those areas are completely blocked off). Match the name on each token to the corresponding area on the map. Return any unused tokens to the box.

walkthrough.title: Place the Game Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Find the Game Round marker and place it on the 1 position of the Round track on the game board. The game lasts up to 10 rounds, so this marker will advance one space each round to track your progress. If anyone reaches 7 Castles or Strongholds before round 10 ends, the game ends immediately.

walkthrough.title: Determine Player Houses
walkthrough.text: Each player now selects which House they wish to control. The six Houses are Stark, Lannister, Greyjoy, Tyrell, Baratheon, and Martell. You can choose freely or assign randomly. If playing with fewer than six players, some Houses are unavailable: five players cannot use Martell, four players cannot use Martell or Tyrell, and three players cannot use Martell, Tyrell, or Greyjoy. Each House has a unique starting position, unique House cards, and a different feel to play.

walkthrough.title: Gather House Materials
walkthrough.text: Each player gathers all materials for their chosen House. This includes: 1 player screen (which has reference info and setup instructions on the inside), 7 House cards (unique characters for combat), 15 Order tokens (10 regular plus 5 special, marked with a star), 1 Supply token, 3 Influence tokens, 1 Victory Point token, 1 Garrison token, and all plastic units of your House color. Each House gets 10 Footmen, 5 Knights, 6 Ships, and 2 Siege Engines for a total of 23 plastic units. Do not take Power tokens yet.

walkthrough.title: Place Influence, Victory, and Supply Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player places their Victory, Supply, and Influence tokens on the game board tracks according to the instructions printed on the inside of their player screen. The starting positions vary by House. Unlike the Influence tracks, multiple Houses can share the same position on the Victory and Supply tracks. If playing with fewer than six players, slide all Influence tokens to the left on each Influence track to fill any empty positions. The Houses in position 1 on each Influence track now claim the corresponding Dominance token: the Iron Throne token, the Valyrian Steel Blade token, or the Messenger Raven token.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Units on the Board
walkthrough.text: Each player now places their starting units on the game board according to the instructions printed on the inside of their player screen. The screen shows exactly which areas get Footmen, Knights, and Ships. Each House starts in different regions of Westeros. For example, Stark starts in the north around Winterfell, Lannister in the west near Lannisport, Baratheon in the southeast near Dragonstone, and so on. Place only the units shown on your screen. The remaining units stay in your play area as reinforcements for future mustering.

walkthrough.title: Place Garrison Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player places their Garrison token on their home area. Your home area is the land area on the board that has your House shield printed on it. For example, Stark places their Garrison on Winterfell, Lannister on Lannisport, Baratheon on Dragonstone, Greyjoy on Pyke, Tyrell on Highgarden, and Martell on Sunspear. Garrison tokens provide extra defensive Combat Strength when your home area is attacked, and they are not units so they do not count toward supply limits.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Power Pool and Gather Starting Power
walkthrough.text: Place all 120 Power tokens (20 per House) in a central pile. This pile is called the Power Pool. Each player then takes 5 Power tokens bearing their House insignia from the Power Pool. These are your starting available power. Power tokens are used for bidding on Influence tracks, defending against wildling attacks, and establishing control of areas you vacate. Keep your available power visible to all players at all times, except just before bidding.

walkthrough.title: King's Court Overlay (3-4 Players Only)
walkthrough.text: If you are playing with three or four players, find the King's Court Overlay token and place it over the first four positions of the King's Court Influence track. This overlay modifies the number of Special Order tokens available to each position, balancing the game for fewer players. In five and six player games, skip this step. The game is now ready to begin. The first round starts by skipping the Westeros Phase and going directly to the Planning Phase.

Only used in 3- and 4-player games to balance Special Order availability
Round 1 skips the Westeros Phase — go directly to the Planning Phase

walkthrough.title: The Game Round Structure
walkthrough.text: A Game of Thrones is played over up to 10 game rounds. Each round consists of three phases in order: the Westeros Phase, the Planning Phase, and the Action Phase. The Westeros Phase is skipped during round 1. After every Action Phase, a new round begins. If any player controls 7 areas containing a Castle or Stronghold at any point, the game ends immediately and that player wins. Otherwise, the game ends after round 10 and the player controlling the most Castles and Strongholds wins.

walkthrough.title: The Westeros Phase
walkthrough.text: The Westeros Phase has four steps. First, advance the Game Round marker one position. Second, draw the top card of all three Westeros decks and reveal them. Third, count any Wildling icons on the three revealed cards and advance the Wildling Threat token that many spaces on the Wildlings track. If the token reaches position 12, a Wildling Attack is immediately resolved. Fourth, resolve the three Westeros cards in order: Deck I, then Deck II, then Deck III. Important Westeros cards include Supply (adjusts army sizes), Mustering (recruit new units), and Clash of Kings (bid for positions on the three Influence tracks).

walkthrough.title: Westeros Card: Supply
walkthrough.text: When a Supply card is drawn, each House counts the Supply icons printed in areas it controls, then moves its Supply token on the Supply track to match. The Supply track shows how many armies you can have and how large each army can be. For example, Supply level 3 allows four armies of sizes 3, 2, 2, and 2. An army is defined as two or more friendly units in the same area. A single unit is not an army and is never affected by supply limits. If your armies exceed your new supply level, you must immediately destroy units until you comply.

walkthrough.title: Westeros Card: Mustering
walkthrough.text: When a Mustering card is drawn, each player in turn order may recruit new units in areas they control that contain a Castle or Stronghold. Strongholds provide 2 mustering points, Castles provide 1. Unit costs are: Footman 1 point, Knight 2 points (or 1 if upgrading from a Footman), Ship 1 point, and Siege Engine 2 points (or 1 if upgrading from a Footman). Ships must be placed in a connected port or adjacent sea area. You cannot muster units that would violate your supply limit. Unused mustering points are lost.

walkthrough.title: Westeros Card: Clash of Kings
walkthrough.text: Clash of Kings triggers bidding for positions on all three Influence tracks. Remove all Influence tokens from the tracks. Bidding is resolved one track at a time: Iron Throne first, then Fiefdoms, then King's Court. For each track, players secretly place Power tokens in a closed fist, then simultaneously reveal. Highest bidder takes position 1, second highest takes position 2, and so on. All ties are decided by the current Iron Throne holder. All Power bid is discarded to the Power Pool regardless of outcome. The player in position 1 on each track claims its Dominance token.

walkthrough.title: The Three Influence Tracks and Dominance Tokens
walkthrough.text: The Iron Throne track determines turn order for everything. Its holder, the Iron Throne token, decides all ties in the game except combat ties and end-game ties. The Fiefdoms track breaks combat ties: the player with the higher position wins. Its holder gets the Valyrian Steel Blade token, which grants +1 Combat Strength once per round. The King's Court track determines how many Special Order tokens each player may use. Its holder gets the Messenger Raven token, which lets them swap one placed order or peek at the top Wildling card after orders are assigned.

walkthrough.title: The Planning Phase
walkthrough.text: During the Planning Phase, all players simultaneously assign Order tokens face-down to every area where they have at least one unit. There are five order types: March, Defense, Support, Raid, and Consolidate Power. Each player has 10 regular orders and 5 special orders (one special of each type). The number of special orders you may use is determined by the number of stars next to your position on the King's Court track. After all orders are placed, they are simultaneously revealed. Then the Messenger Raven holder may swap one order or peek at the top Wildling card.

walkthrough.title: The Action Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: The Action Phase resolves orders in three steps, always cycling through players in turn order. Step 1: Resolve Raid Orders. Step 2: Resolve March Orders (and any resulting combat). Step 3: Resolve Consolidate Power Orders. After step 3, clean up by removing all remaining Support and Defense Orders, standing routed units upright, and flipping the Valyrian Steel Blade and Messenger Raven tokens back to their available sides. In each step, players take turns resolving one order of that type until none remain, then the next step begins.

walkthrough.title: Raid Orders
walkthrough.text: When you resolve a Raid Order, you choose one adjacent enemy Support, Raid, or Consolidate Power Order and remove both that order and your Raid Order. If you raid an enemy Consolidate Power Order, that counts as pillaging: you gain 1 Power token from the Pool and your opponent discards 1 Power token. Raids from land areas cannot target sea areas, but raids from sea areas can target either land or sea. If no eligible adjacent enemy orders exist, the Raid Order is simply removed. The Special Raid Order can additionally remove an adjacent Defense Order.

walkthrough.title: March Orders
walkthrough.text: March Orders are how you move units and start combat. When resolved, you may move all, some, or none of your units from the March Order's area into adjacent areas. Units can split into multiple adjacent areas, but only one of those areas may contain enemy units, triggering one combat per March Order. Land units (Footmen, Knights, Siege Engines) cannot enter sea areas. Ships cannot enter land areas but can move into friendly ports or adjacent seas. Each March Order has a Combat Strength modifier (+1, +0, or -1) that applies when you attack. If you vacate an area, you lose control unless you spend a Power token to establish control.

walkthrough.title: Combat Overview
walkthrough.text: Combat occurs when units march into an area containing enemy units. It resolves in six steps: 1) Call for Support from adjacent areas with Support Orders, 2) Calculate Initial Combat Strength (units + order bonuses + support + garrison), 3) Both players secretly choose and simultaneously reveal a House card from their hand, 4) The Valyrian Steel Blade holder may use it for +1, 5) Calculate Final Combat Strength, 6) Resolve the combat. The higher Final Combat Strength wins. Ties are broken by position on the Fiefdoms track.

walkthrough.title: Unit Combat Strengths
walkthrough.text: Each unit type has a fixed Combat Strength: Footmen contribute 1, Knights contribute 2, Ships contribute 1. Siege Engines contribute 4 Combat Strength but only when attacking or supporting an attack against an area containing a Castle or Stronghold. Otherwise Siege Engines contribute 0. Support Orders in adjacent areas add their units' full Combat Strength to either side. The Special Support Order adds an additional +1 to the supporting Combat Strength.

walkthrough.title: House Cards in Combat
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with 7 unique House cards representing famous characters from A Song of Ice and Fire. Both attacker and defender must secretly select and simultaneously reveal one House card during combat. Each card has a Combat Strength value (added to your total), and either a text ability or combat icons (Swords and Fortifications). Text abilities resolve immediately unless they say otherwise. After combat, both played cards go to their respective discard piles. When you play your last (7th) card, you pick up the other 6 from your discard pile, leaving only the 7th behind.

walkthrough.title: Combat Resolution: Casualties and Retreat
walkthrough.text: The defeated player takes casualties equal to the winner's Sword icons minus the loser's Fortification icons. The defeated player chooses which units to destroy. Then surviving defeated units must retreat. If the attacker lost, units retreat back to where they marched from. If the defender lost, they retreat to one adjacent empty or friendly area (but never to the area the attacker came from). All retreated units are placed on their side to show they are routed. Routed units provide 0 Combat Strength, cannot retreat again (they are destroyed instead), and cannot move. Siege Engines can never retreat and are always destroyed.

walkthrough.title: Consolidate Power Orders
walkthrough.text: In turn order, each player resolves one Consolidate Power Order by removing it and gaining 1 Power token from the Power Pool, plus 1 additional Power token for each Power icon printed in that area. Consolidate Power Orders on sea areas have no effect but may still be placed. The Special Consolidate Power Order has a unique secondary ability: instead of collecting power, it can be used to muster units in its area following normal mustering rules (Stronghold = 2 points, Castle = 1 point). If the area has no Castle or Stronghold, no mustering occurs.

walkthrough.title: Wildling Attacks
walkthrough.text: A Wildling Attack occurs when the Wildling Threat token reaches position 12 or when a Wildling Attack Westeros card is drawn. All players secretly bid Power tokens. The total bid is the Night's Watch strength. If it equals or exceeds the Wildling threat value, the wildlings are defeated and the token resets to 0. The highest bidder receives a reward from the top Wildling card. If the wildlings win, the token drops back only 2 spaces. The lowest bidder suffers a severe penalty and all other players suffer a lesser penalty. All bid Power is discarded regardless of outcome. The Iron Throne holder breaks ties in bidding.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately if any player controls 7 areas with a Castle or Stronghold. Otherwise, it ends after round 10. The player controlling the most Castle and Stronghold areas wins. Tiebreakers are applied in order: most Strongholds, then highest Supply track position, then most available Power tokens, and finally highest Iron Throne track position. Players must always track their Castle and Stronghold count on the Victory track throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Scenario Setup
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with five players controlling House Stark, House Lannister, House Baratheon, House Greyjoy, and House Tyrell. Five players is the recommended count for learning because it keeps all the major regions of Westeros active. Even if you have a different number of players, follow along to see how the phases work. Round 1 is special because it skips the Westeros Phase entirely, going straight to the Planning Phase.

walkthrough.title: Round 1: The Planning Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: Everyone picks up their 15 Order tokens and hides them behind their player screen. Remember, you have 10 regular orders and 5 special orders (marked with a star). Check your position on the King's Court Influence track to see how many special orders you can use this round. Now comes the simultaneous planning: every player must place exactly one Order token face-down on each area where they have units. This is the strategic heart of the game. You are deciding your entire round right now, all at once, without knowing what your opponents will do.

walkthrough.title: Stark's Orders
walkthrough.text: Stark looks at the board. They have units in Winterfell, White Harbor, and The Shivering Sea. Their main concern is expanding southward while keeping the north secure. Stark places a March Order on White Harbor to push into an adjacent territory, a Defense Order on Winterfell since the Garrison already helps here, and a Consolidate Power Order on The Shivering Sea to start building their Power token reserve. Solid opening: expand, defend the home, build resources.

walkthrough.title: Lannister's Orders
walkthrough.text: Lannister eyes the valuable center of the map. They place a March Order on Lannisport to push units into Stoney Sept and the Searoad Marches, a Support Order on Stoney Sept to back up future combats, and a Consolidate Power Order on The Golden Sound. Lannister is thinking ahead — controlling the center gives Supply icons and forward positioning, while the Support Order in Stoney Sept will be crucial if Greyjoy or Stark pushes south.

walkthrough.title: All Orders Revealed
walkthrough.text: Once every player has placed all their orders, everyone flips them face-up simultaneously. This is always an exciting moment: you see your opponents' plans all at once. Greyjoy has March Orders aimed at the western coast. Baratheon is consolidating around Dragonstone and the Narrow Sea. Tyrell has a mix of March and Support orders in the south. Now the Messenger Raven holder gets to swap one order or peek at the Wildling deck. In our scenario, Baratheon holds the Raven and decides to swap a Consolidate Power Order at Dragonstone for a March Order, seeing an opportunity to push north while others are distracted.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Step 1: Resolve Raid Orders
walkthrough.text: The Action Phase begins with Raid Orders. Players resolve them one at a time in turn order, cycling through until no Raid Orders remain. In our scenario, Greyjoy has a Raid Order in Ironman's Bay targeting Lannister's Support Order in an adjacent area. Greyjoy removes both orders from the board. This is devastating — Lannister just lost the support they were counting on for defense. Raiding is how you disrupt opponents' plans before they execute.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Step 2: Resolve March Orders
walkthrough.text: Now we resolve March Orders in turn order. This is where the board changes dramatically. Stark marches a Footman from White Harbor into Moat Cailin, claiming a key area. No enemy units there, so no combat. Lannister marches from Lannisport, splitting units: one Footman to Stoney Sept, one to Searoad Marches. No combat triggered since no enemies are in those areas either. The March Order is removed from the board after resolution.

walkthrough.title: Greyjoy's March: Combat Begins
walkthrough.text: Greyjoy marches two units from Pyke toward the mainland. They move into an area containing a Neutral Force token with strength 3. To defeat a Neutral Force, the marching player must equal or exceed its strength. Greyjoy has a Knight (strength 2) and a Footman (strength 1) with a +0 March Order, totaling 3. That matches the Neutral Force's strength, so the token is destroyed and removed from the game. Greyjoy now controls that area. No House cards are played against Neutral Forces.

walkthrough.title: Baratheon's March: Player vs Player Combat
walkthrough.text: Baratheon marches from Dragonstone into Crackclaw Point, which has a lone Tyrell Footman. Combat ensues. Step 1: Call for Support. There are no Support Orders adjacent, so no support. Step 2: Calculate Initial Combat Strength. Baratheon has a Knight (2) with a +0 March Order, totaling 2. Tyrell has one defending Footman (1). Step 3: Both players secretly pick a House card. Baratheon reveals a card with strength 2. Tyrell reveals a card with strength 1 and one Fortification icon.

walkthrough.title: Combat Resolution
walkthrough.text: Step 4: Neither player has the Valyrian Steel Blade in this scenario. Step 5: Final Combat Strength. Baratheon has 2 (Knight) + 2 (House card) = 4. Tyrell has 1 (Footman) + 1 (House card) = 2. Step 6: Baratheon wins. Check Sword icons: Baratheon's card has 1 Sword. Tyrell's card has 1 Fortification, which cancels that Sword. So Tyrell takes zero casualties. However, Tyrell's surviving Footman must retreat. Tyrell retreats the Footman to an adjacent empty area and lays it on its side to show it is routed. Baratheon moves into Crackclaw Point.

walkthrough.title: Tyrell's Mistake: Forgetting to Support
walkthrough.text: Looking back at Tyrell's orders, they had a Support Order in an area adjacent to Crackclaw Point but forgot that support must be called during combat, not placed during combat. The mistake was not placing the Support Order adjacent to where the battle would actually happen. Tyrell put their Support Order next to Highgarden instead, which was not adjacent to the combat zone. In this game, you need to anticipate where fights will happen and position Support Orders accordingly during the Planning Phase, not react to battles after they start.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Support Before You Need It
walkthrough.text: Support is the most important order in the game for both defense and offense. A lone Footman with two adjacent Support Orders can be nearly unbeatable. Always think about where enemies might attack and place your Support Orders adjacent to those vulnerable areas. Support can also be offered to other players' combats, which makes it the foundation of alliances and negotiation.

walkthrough.title: Tyrell's Second Mistake: Overextending
walkthrough.text: Tyrell also marched units from Highgarden toward the east, leaving only a Garrison behind. They were eager to grab territory but spread their forces thin. Now Highgarden, their home area, has no units defending it — only the Garrison token. While the Garrison adds Combat Strength on defense, it does not count as a unit for supply, cannot be assigned orders, and if defeated, it is permanently removed from the game. Leaving your home area undefended is one of the riskiest moves in the game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Protect Your Home Area
walkthrough.text: Your Garrison token is permanent defense but it is not a replacement for actual units. If an enemy takes your home area, you lose the Garrison forever and you lose the ability to muster there. Never leave your home area undefended unless you are absolutely certain no enemy can reach it this round. A March Order can chain through multiple areas via staggering, so threats can come from surprisingly far away.

walkthrough.title: Tyrell's Third Mistake: Ignoring Supply
walkthrough.text: By marching aggressively, Tyrell has now created three groups of two units each. But their Supply level only allows armies of 2, 2, and 2. If they pick up even one more unit through mustering, they will need to carefully manage where they place it. Worse, if a Supply Westeros card is drawn next round and Tyrell has lost control of supply-icon areas due to overextension, they might be forced to destroy units. Always keep your supply limits in mind when spreading out.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Step 3: Resolve Consolidate Power Orders
walkthrough.text: Now we resolve Consolidate Power Orders. Stark resolves their Consolidate Power in The Shivering Sea. Since it is a sea area, the Consolidate Power Order has no effect. This is a common beginner trap. You can place a Consolidate Power Order on a sea area, but you will get nothing from it. Baratheon, on the other hand, had Consolidate Power on Dragonstone before swapping it. Other players who kept their Consolidate Power Orders on land areas with Power icons gain bonus Power tokens.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Consolidate Power Wisely
walkthrough.text: Power tokens are the currency of diplomacy and survival. You need them to bid on Influence tracks during Clash of Kings, to defend against Wildling attacks, and to establish control of vacated areas. Do not waste Consolidate Power Orders on sea areas. Place them on land areas with printed Power icons for maximum return. Areas like King's Landing and Dragonstone have Power icons and are excellent Consolidate Power locations.

walkthrough.title: Clean Up and End of Round 1
walkthrough.text: After all Consolidate Power Orders are resolved, the Clean Up step begins. Remove all remaining Support and Defense Orders from the board. Stand any routed units upright. Flip the Valyrian Steel Blade and Messenger Raven tokens back to their available sides. The first round is complete. Starting with round 2, every round will begin with the Westeros Phase, where three Westeros cards will shape the round ahead.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 Preview: The Westeros Phase
walkthrough.text: Round 2 begins with the Westeros Phase. Advance the Round marker to position 2. Draw one card from each Westeros deck. Suppose we draw a Supply card from Deck I, a Mustering card from Deck II, and a card with a Wildling icon from Deck III. First, advance the Wildling Threat token one space. Then resolve the cards in order. Supply adjusts everyone's army limits. Mustering lets you recruit new units at Castles and Strongholds. This is where the game starts to accelerate — armies grow, the wildling threat rises, and the board gets more contested.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen all three phases in action, experienced your first combat, watched Tyrell make three common mistakes — poor Support placement, overextending from home, and ignoring supply limits — and completed a full round. Remember: plan your Support Orders next to where you expect combat, keep your home area defended, mind your supply limits, and do not waste Consolidate Power on sea areas. You are ready to continue playing on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Claim the Iron Throne
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common mistakes new players make.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every order type, unit type, House card, and Westeros event.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Valar Morghulis!

summary.title: Welcome and Scenario Setup
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 5 Houses: Stark, Lannister, Baratheon, Greyjoy, Tyrell
5 players is recommended for learning — all major regions active
Round 1 skips the Westeros Phase

summary.title: Round 1: The Planning Phase Begins
bullets: All players place Order tokens face-down simultaneously
10 regular + 5 special orders per player (special count limited by King's Court position)
You decide your entire round during this phase

summary.title: Stark's Orders
bullets: March on White Harbor (expand south), Defense on Winterfell (protect home)
Consolidate Power on The Shivering Sea (build resources)
Solid opening: expand, defend, accumulate Power

summary.title: Lannister's Orders
bullets: March from Lannisport to claim central areas
Support Order to back up future combats
Consolidate Power in The Golden Sound for income

summary.title: All Orders Revealed
bullets: All orders flipped face-up simultaneously
Messenger Raven holder (Baratheon) may swap 1 order or peek at Wildling deck
First view of everyone's plans creates dramatic tension

summary.title: Action Phase Step 1: Resolve Raid Orders
bullets: Resolve in turn order, one Raid at a time
Greyjoy raids Lannister's Support Order — devastating disruption
Raiding cancels opponents' plans before they execute

summary.title: Action Phase Step 2: Resolve March Orders
bullets: March Orders move units and claim territory
No combat if marching into empty areas or areas with only your units
March Order removed from board after resolution

summary.title: Greyjoy's March: Combat Begins
bullets: Greyjoy attacks a Neutral Force (strength 3)
Knight (2) + Footman (1) + March +0 = 3, matches Neutral Force strength
No House cards played vs Neutral Forces; token destroyed on success

summary.title: Baratheon's March: Player vs Player Combat
bullets: Baratheon marches into area with Tyrell's Footman — combat ensues
Step 1-3: No support available, calculate strengths, choose House cards

summary.title: Combat Resolution
bullets: Baratheon wins (4 vs 2 Final Combat Strength)
1 Sword - 1 Fortification = 0 casualties for Tyrell
Tyrell's Footman retreats and is routed (placed on its side)

summary.title: Tyrell's Mistake: Forgetting to Support
bullets: Support Order was not adjacent to the actual combat area
Must anticipate where fights will happen during Planning Phase
Cannot react to battles after they start

summary.title: Lesson: Plan Support Before You Need It
bullets: Support is the most important order for both offense and defense
A lone Footman with 2 adjacent Support Orders can be nearly unbeatable
Support can also be offered to other players' combats — key to alliances

summary.title: Tyrell's Second Mistake: Overextending
bullets: Tyrell left Highgarden defended only by Garrison token (no units)
Garrison is permanent but if defeated, it is gone forever
Losing your home area = losing mustering capability there

summary.title: Lesson: Protect Your Home Area
bullets: Garrison token is not a substitute for actual defending units
March Orders can chain through multiple areas — threats come from far away
Losing your home Garrison is permanent and devastating

summary.title: Tyrell's Third Mistake: Ignoring Supply
bullets: Overextension created armies exceeding supply limits
A future Supply card could force unit destruction
Always check supply limits before spreading forces

summary.title: Action Phase Step 3: Resolve Consolidate Power Orders
bullets: Gain 1 Power per order + 1 per Power icon in the area
Consolidate Power on sea areas has NO effect — common trap
Place on land areas with Power icons for maximum return

summary.title: Lesson: Consolidate Power Wisely
bullets: Power tokens fund bidding, wildling defense, and area control
Best on land areas with printed Power icons (e.g., King's Landing, Dragonstone)
Never waste on sea areas

summary.title: Clean Up and End of Round 1
bullets: Remove remaining Support/Defense Orders from the board
Stand routed units upright, flip Valyrian Steel Blade and Messenger Raven to available
Round 1 complete — round 2 starts with the Westeros Phase

summary.title: Round 2 Preview: The Westeros Phase
bullets: Draw 3 Westeros cards, advance Wildling Threat for each Wildling icon
Supply adjusts army limits, Mustering lets you recruit new units
Game accelerates: armies grow, wildling threat rises, board gets contested

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Experienced all 3 phases and first combat
Watched Tyrell's 3 mistakes: bad Support placement, overextending, ignoring supply
Key lessons: plan Support near combat zones, defend home, mind supply limits

summary.title: Before You Claim the Iron Throne
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes
Reference the Appendix for every order, unit, and event
Ask your personal GM Guide in the Q&A tab for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that sink new players most often. First, neglecting Power tokens. You need them for bidding on Influence tracks, surviving Wildling attacks, and establishing area control. If you spend all your Power early, you are defenseless. Second, leaving your home area undefended. Losing your Garrison token is permanent and losing your Stronghold is devastating for mustering. Third, ignoring your supply limits when expanding. You can march into new territory all day, but if your armies exceed supply, a Supply card will force you to destroy units. Fourth, forgetting that the Westeros Phase is skipped in round 1, then being surprised when Supply and Mustering hit in round 2.

walkthrough.title: The Importance of Diplomacy
walkthrough.text: This game is won and lost through alliances as much as through combat. You cannot fight on every front at once. Before each Planning Phase, talk to your neighbors. Agree on non-aggression pacts, coordinate Support Orders, and negotiate temporary truces. But remember: alliances are never binding. Anyone can break a promise at any time. The best diplomats make deals that benefit both sides so the other player has no reason to betray them. Always have a backup plan in case an ally turns on you.

walkthrough.title: Power Token Economy
walkthrough.text: Power tokens are the most underappreciated resource in the game. You start with only 5 and need them for Clash of Kings bidding (which can cost 4 to 6 tokens to win a single track), Wildling defense (where bidding nothing risks severe penalties), and establishing control of vacated areas (1 token per area). Build your Power through Consolidate Power Orders on land areas with Power icons, the Game of Thrones Westeros card, and pillaging via Raid Orders on enemy Consolidate Power tokens. Aim to have at least 5 Power at all times as emergency reserves.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Influence Tracks
walkthrough.text: Each Influence track provides a different advantage, and all three matter. The Iron Throne position sets turn order, which is critical because resolving March Orders first lets you grab territory before opponents can react. The Fiefdoms position breaks combat ties and provides the Valyrian Steel Blade (+1 strength once per round), which is game-changing in close battles. The King's Court position determines how many Special Order tokens you can use, and Special Orders are significantly stronger than regular ones. Evaluate which track matters most to your current strategy before bidding during Clash of Kings.

walkthrough.title: Support Order Strategy
walkthrough.text: Support Orders are the most versatile and powerful order type. A Support Order lets your units add their Combat Strength to any adjacent combat, whether you are involved or not. This means one well-placed army with a Support Order can defend multiple areas simultaneously without moving. It also means you can offer support to another player's battle as a diplomatic tool. Key tips: Support Orders are not removed after combat, so one Support Order can support multiple adjacent battles in the same round. Always place Support Orders adjacent to where you expect combat, not on safe interior areas.

walkthrough.title: When to Attack vs Defend
walkthrough.text: Knowing when to push and when to hold is the most important skill in this game. Attack when you have a clear numerical advantage plus support, when your opponent's Support Orders have been raided, or when you can take a Castle or Stronghold that will bring you closer to 7. Defend when you are outgunned, when you hold critical territory with good Defense Orders and Garrison bonuses, or when you want an opponent to waste their March Order attacking your well-supported position. Remember that the defender has the advantage in ties if they are higher on the Fiefdoms track.

walkthrough.title: House Card Management
walkthrough.text: You have 7 House cards and you will use all of them before getting them back. Think of your House card hand as a limited resource over 7 combats. Do not play your strongest card in an unimportant skirmish. Save high-strength cards for decisive battles over Castles and Strongholds. Study your opponents' discard piles to know what they have left. If an opponent has already played their best cards, that is the time to press an attack. And remember: when you play your 7th card, you get the other 6 back, but the 7th stays discarded. Try to make your weakest card the last one you play.

walkthrough.title: Ship Transport and Naval Control
walkthrough.text: Ship transport is one of the most powerful mechanics in the game and new players often overlook it. Ships in consecutive adjacent sea areas form a bridge that lets land units move directly from one coastal area to another in a single March Order. This means a player with a chain of ships across two or three sea areas can attack from across the map in one move. Control of key sea areas is essential. Also, taking control of an enemy's port lets you replace their ships with your own, which can cripple their naval capabilities instantly.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Do not spend all Power tokens early — you need them for bidding and wildling defense
Never leave your home area undefended — losing your Garrison is permanent
Always check supply limits before expanding armies
Remember: Westeros Phase is skipped in round 1

summary.title: The Importance of Diplomacy
bullets: You cannot fight on every front — make alliances with neighbors
Alliances are never binding — anyone can betray at any time
Make deals that benefit both sides so allies have reason to stay loyal
Always have a backup plan for betrayal

summary.title: Power Token Economy
bullets: Start with 5 Power — need for Clash of Kings, Wildlings, and area control
Build Power via Consolidate Power Orders, Game of Thrones card, and pillaging
Aim to keep at least 5 Power in reserve at all times

summary.title: Understanding the Influence Tracks
bullets: Iron Throne: turn order (act first = grab territory first)
Fiefdoms: combat tiebreaker + Valyrian Steel Blade (+1 once/round)
King's Court: Special Order count + Messenger Raven (swap order or peek at Wildlings)

summary.title: Support Order Strategy
bullets: Support adds full unit strength to any adjacent combat
Support Orders are NOT removed after combat — can support multiple battles per round
Place adjacent to expected combat zones, not on safe interior areas
Offering support to others is a key diplomatic tool

summary.title: When to Attack vs Defend
bullets: Attack with clear advantage + support, or after raiding enemy Support
Defend when outgunned or holding key territory with Defense Orders + Garrison
Defender wins ties if higher on Fiefdoms track
Bait opponents into attacking well-supported positions

summary.title: House Card Management
bullets: 7 House cards total — treat as a limited resource over 7 combats
Save strong cards for decisive Castle/Stronghold battles
Study opponent discard piles to know what they have left
Try to play your weakest card last (7th card stays discarded)

summary.title: Ship Transport and Naval Control
bullets: Ships in consecutive sea areas form a transport bridge for land units
One March Order can move land units across multiple sea areas
Capturing enemy ports replaces their ships with yours
Naval control is essential for both offense and long-range defense

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Influence Tracks
walkthrough.text: Advanced players know that the Influence tracks are where games are won. The Iron Throne track determines turn order, and going first in the Resolve March Orders step is enormous because you can claim territory or attack before opponents can respond. The Fiefdoms track determines combat tiebreakers, and in a game full of close combats, position 1 with the Valyrian Steel Blade can mean winning every 50/50 fight. The King's Court track gives you more Special Orders, which are strictly better than their regular versions. Budget your Power for Clash of Kings carefully and prioritize the track that fits your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Staggered March Chains
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful offensive techniques is the staggered march. Place March Orders in multiple adjacent areas. When you resolve the first March Order, move units into the area containing your second March Order. Later, when you resolve that second March Order, those same units move again. This lets units effectively cover two or even three areas in a single round. The danger is that an opponent might raid or attack one of the links in your chain before you resolve it, breaking the entire sequence. Use this technique when you have early turn order to minimize that risk.

walkthrough.title: The Special Consolidate Power Muster
walkthrough.text: The Special Consolidate Power Order can muster units instead of collecting Power. This is incredibly powerful because it lets you muster outside of the Mustering Westeros card, giving you units when opponents do not expect them. Place it on areas with Castles or Strongholds for surprise reinforcements. Since this happens during the Consolidate Power step (step 3 of the Action Phase), enemies cannot raid it during the Raid step — only Special Raid Orders can remove it. Time this to suddenly appear with extra forces when your opponent thought they were safe.

walkthrough.title: Wildling Attack Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Smart players use the Wildling threat strategically. When the Wildling Threat token is high (near 12), the collective bid to defeat them must be large, draining everyone's Power. If you have the Messenger Raven, peek at the top Wildling card. If the penalty for losing would hurt your opponents more than you, consider bidding zero and letting the wildlings win. The highest bidder gets a reward even if the Night's Watch wins, so bid high when you want the reward and the group seems ready to cooperate. The lowest bidder takes the severe penalty when the wildlings win, so never be the lowest bidder unless you can afford the consequences.

walkthrough.title: House Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Each House has a spread of House cards from powerful to weak. Advanced players track exactly which cards opponents have available by watching their discard piles. If Lannister has already used Tywin (strength 4) and The Hound (strength 3), you know their remaining cards are weaker. That is when you launch your attack. Conversely, save your own powerhouse cards for the battles that matter most. Some cards have text abilities that cancel or ignore enemy card effects. These are most effective when used to nullify an opponent's best card, turning their strength against them. The card cycling mechanic — pick up 6 when you play your 7th — also means timing your weak card to be the 7th play.

walkthrough.title: Port and Naval Strategy
walkthrough.text: Ports are one of the most strategically important features on the board. You can muster ships directly into a port even when enemy ships control the connected sea area. This makes ports nearly impossible to fully blockade. Use this to rebuild a fleet behind enemy lines. When you take control of an enemy's land area connected to a port, you can replace their ships with your own, instantly flipping naval control. Target enemy ports when you want to cripple their naval capability. Ships in ports can raid and support the adjacent sea area, making them excellent defensive pickets.

walkthrough.title: Supply Track Warfare
walkthrough.text: Supply is a weapon. When a Supply Westeros card is drawn, every player recalculates based on areas they currently control. If you can take territory containing Supply icons just before a Supply card appears, you gain larger armies while your opponent shrinks. Similarly, you can force an opponent into a supply crisis by taking their Supply-icon areas. At Supply level 2, you can only have three armies of sizes 3, 2, and 2. At Supply level 0, you can have only two armies of 2 units each. Knowing the Supply track limits by heart lets you plan invasions that will cripple opponents even if a Supply card does not appear for several rounds.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Positioning
walkthrough.text: The game ends after round 10 or when someone hits 7 Castles/Strongholds. In the late game, count everyone's Castle/Stronghold count obsessively. If someone is at 5 or 6, the entire table should be working to stop them. If you are in the lead, keep a low profile and strike on the last round when opponents cannot respond. If the game is going to round 10, remember the tiebreakers: Strongholds first, then Supply, then Power, then Iron Throne position. Controlling Strongholds (worth 2 mustering points each) is strictly better than Castles in a tiebreak. Position yourself to win the tiebreaker chain if you expect a close finish.

walkthrough.title: House-Specific Opening Strategies
walkthrough.text: Each House has a natural strategic direction. Stark starts isolated in the north with strong defense but must expand south through Moat Cailin. Lannister controls the rich center but is surrounded by threats from Greyjoy, Stark, Tyrell, and Baratheon. Greyjoy has strong naval capability and should dominate western sea areas early. Baratheon has a Stronghold on Dragonstone and can push into the Narrow Sea or King's Landing. Tyrell starts in the fertile south and should secure supply before pushing outward. Martell sits in the far south with excellent defensive terrain but must expand before being boxed in. Know your House's strengths and play to them.

walkthrough.title: The Raid-Support Meta
walkthrough.text: At the highest levels of play, the game revolves around the interaction between Raid and Support Orders. Support is the best defensive order because it can bolster adjacent areas without moving. But Raid can remove Support Orders before they activate, leaving defenders exposed. The Special Raid Order can even remove Defense Orders. This creates a layered planning puzzle: you want to Support key areas, but you need to protect those Support Orders from raids, which might mean placing a Defense Order on the supporting area. Understanding this Raid-Support dynamic and predicting where your opponent will Raid is the deepest skill in the game.

summary.title: Controlling the Influence Tracks
bullets: Iron Throne: early turn order means claiming territory before opponents can respond
Fiefdoms: position 1 + Valyrian Steel Blade wins every close combat
King's Court: more Special Orders = strictly better order options
Budget Power for Clash of Kings based on which track matters most to your strategy

summary.title: Staggered March Chains
bullets: Place March Orders in consecutive adjacent areas
Resolve them in sequence to move units 2-3 areas in one round
Risk: enemy raids or attacks can break the chain
Use when you have early turn order to minimize risk

summary.title: The Special Consolidate Power Muster
bullets: Special CP Order can muster units instead of collecting Power
Surprise reinforcements outside of Mustering Westeros cards
Resolved in step 3 of Action Phase — safe from normal Raids
Place on Castles/Strongholds for maximum muster points

summary.title: Wildling Attack Manipulation
bullets: Use Messenger Raven to peek at Wildling card and plan bid accordingly
High threat = large collective bid required, draining everyone's Power
Bid high when you want the reward; never be lowest bidder if wildlings might win
Sometimes letting wildlings win strategically can hurt opponents more than you

summary.title: House Card Timing
bullets: Track opponent discard piles to know remaining card strength
Attack when opponent has used their strongest cards
Save cancel/ignore abilities for opponent's best cards
Time your weakest card to be the 7th play (stays discarded)

summary.title: Port and Naval Strategy
bullets: Ports allow mustering ships even when enemy controls the connected sea
Capturing enemy land area + port replaces their ships with yours
Port ships can raid and support the adjacent sea area
Ports are nearly impossible to fully blockade — exploit this

summary.title: Supply Track Warfare
bullets: Take enemy Supply-icon areas before a Supply card hits
Supply level 0 allows only two armies of 2 units each
Memorize Supply track limits to plan crippling invasions
Supply is a weapon — shrink opponents' armies by taking their land

summary.title: Endgame Positioning
bullets: Count everyone's Castle/Stronghold totals constantly
If ahead: stay quiet, strike on the last round when opponents cannot respond
Tiebreakers: Strongholds > Supply > Power > Iron Throne position
Strongholds are strictly better than Castles in every way

summary.title: House-Specific Opening Strategies
bullets: Stark: strong defense, must expand south through Moat Cailin
Lannister: rich center, surrounded by threats on all sides
Greyjoy: naval dominance, control western seas early
Baratheon: Dragonstone Stronghold, push Narrow Sea or King's Landing
Tyrell: fertile south, secure supply before expanding
Martell: defensive terrain, must expand before being boxed in

summary.title: The Raid-Support Meta
bullets: Support is best defense; Raid removes Support before it activates
Special Raid can remove Defense Orders too
Protect Support Orders from Raids with layered defensive planning
Predicting where opponents will Raid is the deepest skill in the game

entries.definition: A land unit that contributes 1 Combat Strength. Costs 1 mustering point. Each House has 10 Footmen. Can be upgraded to a Knight or Siege Engine for 1 mustering point. Cannot enter sea areas or ports.
A land unit that contributes 2 Combat Strength. Costs 2 mustering points (or 1 to upgrade from a Footman). Each House has 5 Knights. Cannot enter sea areas or ports.

A naval unit that contributes 1 Combat Strength. Costs 1 mustering point. Each House has 6 Ships. Ships move between sea areas and friendly ports. Ships form transport bridges for land units across consecutive sea areas.

A land unit that contributes 4 Combat Strength when attacking or supporting an attack against an area with a Castle or Stronghold. Otherwise contributes 0. Costs 2 mustering points (or 1 to upgrade from a Footman). Each House has 2. Cannot retreat — always destroyed if forced to retreat.

Order token that moves units to adjacent areas. Two regular March Orders per House: one with +0 and one with -1 Combat Strength modifier. Only way to take new territory and initiate combat. One combat per March Order.

Order token that provides +1 Combat Strength bonus to the defender. Two regular Defense Orders per House. Remains on the board until removed and provides its bonus against any number of attacks in the same round.

Order token that allows units in the area to add their Combat Strength to any adjacent combat. Two regular Support Orders per House. Not removed after use — can support multiple adjacent combats in the same round. Can support either side in a combat.

Order token that removes one adjacent enemy Support, Raid, or Consolidate Power Order. Two regular Raid Orders per House. Raiding a Consolidate Power Order counts as pillaging (raider gains 1 Power, opponent loses 1). Land raids cannot target sea areas.
Order token that gains 1 Power token plus 1 per Power icon printed in the area. Two regular Consolidate Power Orders per House. Has no effect on sea areas. Resolved during step 3 of the Action Phase.
Star-marked March Order with a +1 Combat Strength modifier instead of +0 or -1. One per House. Requires King's Court track stars to use.
Star-marked Defense Order providing +2 Combat Strength instead of +1. One per House. Requires King's Court track stars to use.
Star-marked Support Order that adds +1 Combat Strength on top of the supporting units' strength. One per House. Requires King's Court track stars to use.
Star-marked Raid Order that can remove an adjacent Defense Order in addition to normal Raid targets (Support, Raid, Consolidate Power). One per House. Requires King's Court track stars to use.

Star-marked Consolidate Power Order that can alternatively be used to muster units in its area (following normal mustering rules) instead of collecting Power. One per House. Requires King's Court track stars to use. Cannot muster if placed on a port.

Dominance token held by the player in position 1 on the Iron Throne Influence track. The holder decides the outcome of all ties in the game except combat ties (decided by Fiefdoms track) and end-game ties (decided by tiebreaker chain).

Dominance token held by the player in position 1 on the Fiefdoms Influence track. Once per round, the holder may add +1 Combat Strength to their side in combat. Flipped to its faded side when used and refreshed at the end of the Action Phase.

Dominance token held by the player in position 1 on the King's Court Influence track. At the end of the Assign Orders step, the holder may either swap one placed Order token for an unused one, or look at the top card of the Wildling deck (and optionally move it to the bottom). Flipped when used and refreshed at end of Action Phase.

Determines turn order for all game mechanics. The player in position 1 holds the Iron Throne token and breaks all non-combat ties. Position is set during setup and can change via the Clash of Kings Westeros card.
Determines combat tiebreakers. The player higher on this track wins ties in combat. The player in position 1 holds the Valyrian Steel Blade token.

Determines how many Special Order tokens each player may use during the Planning Phase. The number of stars printed next to each position indicates the limit. Position 1 holds the Messenger Raven token. In 3-4 player games, the King's Court Overlay modifies the available stars.
"Winter is Coming." Starts in the north around Winterfell. Strong defensive position, isolated from early conflict but must expand south. Home area: Winterfell. Available in 3+ player games.
"Hear Me Roar." Starts in the west around Lannisport. Rich central position with many Supply icons but surrounded by threats on all sides. Home area: Lannisport. Available in 3+ player games.

"Ours is the Fury." Starts in the southeast around Dragonstone. Has a Stronghold at Dragonstone and can push into the Narrow Sea or toward King's Landing. Home area: Dragonstone. Available in 3+ player games.
"We Do Not Sow." Starts on the Iron Islands (Pyke). Strong naval capability with multiple ships. Should dominate western sea areas early. Home area: Pyke. Available in 4+ player games.

"Growing Strong." Starts in the fertile south around Highgarden. Excellent supply potential and mustering. Should secure the south before pushing outward. Home area: Highgarden. Available in 5+ player games.

"Unbowed, Unbent, Unbroken." Starts in the far south in Dorne around Sunspear. Excellent defensive terrain but must expand before being boxed in. Home area: Sunspear. Available in 6-player games only.

The first phase of each game round (skipped in round 1). Draw one card from each of three Westeros decks and resolve them in order. The Wildling Threat token advances for each Wildling icon on drawn cards.

The second phase of each game round. All players simultaneously assign Order tokens face-down to areas with units. After revealing, the Messenger Raven holder may swap one order or peek at the Wildling deck. Three steps: Assign Orders, Reveal Orders, Use Messenger Raven.

The third phase of each game round. Orders are resolved in three steps: Raid Orders, March Orders (with combat), then Consolidate Power Orders. Each step cycles through turn order until no orders of that type remain. Ends with Clean Up.
Westeros card that forces all players to recount Supply icons in controlled areas and adjust their Supply track position. Players exceeding their new supply limits must immediately destroy units.
Westeros card that allows each player in turn order to recruit new units at Castles (1 mustering point) and Strongholds (2 mustering points). Units must comply with supply limits.
Westeros card that triggers bidding for all three Influence tracks. Bidding proceeds one track at a time: Iron Throne, Fiefdoms, then King's Court. All bid Power is discarded.
Westeros card that lets players collect Power tokens for each Power icon in areas they control, plus 1 Power for each friendly Port with a Ship unit (as long as the connected sea has no enemy Ships).
Westeros card that triggers an immediate Wildling Attack, regardless of the current Wildling Threat level.

Tracks the strength of the wildling threat on the Wildlings track (0-12). Advances when Westeros cards with Wildling icons are drawn. Triggers an automatic Wildling Attack at position 12. Resets to 0 if the Night's Watch wins, drops 2 if the wildlings win.

All players simultaneously bid Power tokens. Total bid = Night's Watch strength. If it equals or exceeds the Wildling threat, players win and the highest bidder gets a reward. If wildlings win, lowest bidder suffers severe penalty and others suffer lesser penalties. All bid Power discarded.

9 cards in the Wildling deck, each with a Night's Watch Victory reward and a Wildling Victory penalty (severe for lowest bidder, lesser for others). Drawn when a Wildling Attack is resolved. Placed at the bottom of the deck after use.
A fortification printed on certain land areas on the game board. Provides 1 mustering point during mustering. Controlling areas with Castles and Strongholds is how you win the game.

A fortification printed on certain land areas, more powerful than a Castle. Provides 2 mustering points during mustering. Strongholds are the tiebreaker for winning the game and are worth more strategically.

Special area between a land area and its adjacent sea area. Only the controller of the connected land area may use the port. Maximum 3 ships in a port. Ships can be mustered in ports even if enemy ships control the connected sea. Capturing the connected land area lets the new controller replace enemy port ships with their own.

Track on the game board that determines how many armies each player can have and the maximum size of each army. Adjusted when a Supply Westeros card is drawn. An army is 2+ units in one area; single units are not armies.
Track on the game board where players record the number of areas they control containing Castles and/or Strongholds. Reaching position 7 triggers an instant win.
Track on the game board numbered 1-10. The Game Round marker advances one position at the start of each Westeros Phase. The game ends after round 10 if no player has achieved 7 Castles/Strongholds.

Tokens bearing each House's insignia, used as currency for bidding on Influence tracks, bidding during Wildling Attacks, and establishing control of vacated areas. 20 per House. Stored in the Power Pool when not in player hands.

Central pile containing all Power tokens not currently held by players. When players discard Power or lose bids, tokens return here. When players gain Power, they take tokens bearing their House insignia from here.

Defensive token placed on each House's home area during setup. Adds its printed Combat Strength value when the home area is attacked. Not a unit — does not count for supply, cannot be assigned orders. If defeated, permanently removed from the game.

Token placed during setup representing independent areas. Players must equal or exceed the token's printed strength to destroy it and claim the area. No House cards are played against Neutral Forces. Tokens marked with ~ are impassable. 14 total tokens with different player count ranges.

7 unique character cards per House used in combat. Both attacker and defender must play one card per combat. Cards have Combat Strength, text abilities, and/or Sword and Fortification icons. Played cards go to discard. When the 7th card is played, pick up the other 6.

Combat icon on House cards and Tides of Battle cards. The winner counts Sword icons on their card. Each Sword forces the loser to destroy one unit in the embattled area, reduced by the loser's Fortification icons.
Combat icon on House cards and Tides of Battle cards. Each Fortification icon on the loser's card cancels one of the winner's Sword icons, reducing casualties.

A unit placed on its side after retreating from combat. Provides 0 Combat Strength but still counts for supply. If forced to retreat again, it is destroyed instead. Cannot move, even with a March Order. Stood upright during Clean Up.

The total strength a player brings to combat. Includes unit Combat Strength, Order modifiers (March/Defense), Support, Garrison, House card strength, Valyrian Steel Blade (+1), and Tides of Battle modifiers. Highest total wins; ties broken by Fiefdoms track.

The sequence in which players act, determined by position on the Iron Throne Influence track. Position 1 acts first, position 2 second, etc. Used for resolving orders, mustering, supply adjustments, and all other sequential game mechanics.
Two or more friendly units in the same land or sea area. A single unit is not an army and is unaffected by supply limits. Army number and size are restricted by the Supply track.

A land area containing a House's printed shield on the game board. Each House has one home area. Control automatically reverts to the original House if an occupying player vacates without establishing control. Contains the Garrison token at game start.

When vacating a land area via March Order, a player may spend 1 Power token to place it in the area, maintaining control. Only possible when vacating with a March Order. Power tokens on the board are removed when an opponent takes the area.

When a Raid Order removes an enemy Consolidate Power Order, the raiding player gains 1 Power token from the Pool and the opponent discards 1 Power token. The raider always gains Power even if the opponent has none to lose.

Ships in consecutive adjacent sea areas form a bridge allowing land units (Footmen, Knights, Siege Engines) to move from one coastal area to another in a single March Order. No limit to transport uses per round. Cannot use another player's ships. Ships in ports or with any order can serve as transport.
A geographic boundary on the game board that prevents adjacency between the two land areas it separates. Areas divided by a river cannot march, support, or raid each other.
An exception to the river rule. Two land areas separated by a river but connected by a bridge are considered adjacent for all game purposes.

Three special land areas (Dragonstone, Pyke, The Arbor) completely surrounded by sea. Still land areas that follow normal land rules, but Footmen, Knights, and Siege Engines must use Ship Transport to reach them.

24 small cards that add randomness to combat. After House cards are revealed, each combatant draws one Tides of Battle card, adding its Combat Strength modifier and Sword/Fortification icons. The Valyrian Steel Blade can be used to redraw. Skull icons force the opposing player to take one extra casualty regardless of who wins.

Icon on certain Tides of Battle cards. If a player's drawn Tides of Battle card has a Skull icon, the opposing player must suffer one additional casualty. This casualty cannot be prevented by Fortification icons. Garrisons are immune to Skull icons.
A token placed over the King's Court Influence track in 3- and 4-player games. Modifies the number of Special Order stars available at each position to balance the game for fewer players.

House-specific reference screen that shows Order token types, starting unit placement, starting track positions, and setup instructions. Used to hide unused Order tokens and Power tokens during bidding.
